greek_head: Ὦ φίλε Φαῖδρε ποῖ δὴ καὶ πόθεν Παρὰ Λυσίου ὦ Σώκρατες τοῦ Κεφάλου πορεύομαι
greek_tokens: eimi filos faidros poi dh kai pinw para lusios eimi swkraths o kefalos poreuw de pros peripatos ecw teixos suxnos gar ekei diatribw xronos kaqhmai ek ewqinos o de sos kai emos etairos peiqw akoumenos kata o odos poiew o peripatos fhmi gar akopos eimi o en o
english_head: Dear Phaedrus, whither away, and where do you come from? From Lysias, Socrates, the son of Cephalus; and I am going for a walk outside the wall. For I spent a long time there with Lysias, sitting since early morning; and on the advice of your friend and mine, Acumenus, I am taking my walk on the roads; for he says they are less fatiguing

greek_head: δρόμοις Καλῶς γάρ ὦ ἑταῖρε λέγει ἀτὰρ Λυσίας ἦν ὡς
greek_tokens: dromos kalos gar eimi etairos legw atar lusios eimi ws eoika en astu nai para epikratew en ode o pimplhmi o olumpias oikia o moruxia tis oun dh eimi o diatribh h dhlos ostis o logos su lusios estiaw peusis ei su sxolh proeimi akouw tis de ou an oiomai egw kata pindaros kai asxolia uper pragma poiew o teos te kai lusios diatribh akew
english_head: than the streets. He is right, my friend. Then Lysias, it seems,was in the city? Yes, at Epicrates’ house, the one that belonged to Morychus, near the Olympieum. What was your conversation? But it is obvious that Lysias entertained you with his speeches. You shall hear, if you have leisure to walk along and listen. What? Don’t you believe that I consider hearing your conversation with Lysias a greater thing even than business,Pind. Isthm 1.1as Pindar says?

greek_head: Πρόαγε δή Λέγοις ἄν
greek_tokens: proagw dh legos an kai meis eimi swkraths prosakouw ge su o akoh o gar su logos eimi peri os diatribw ou oida ostis tropos erwtikos grafw gar dh o lusios peiraw tis o kalh ou upo erasths de alla autos dh outos kai komyeuw legw gar ws xaristeos mh eraw mallon h eraw w gennaios eiqe grafw ws xrh penhs mallon h plousios kai presbus h neos kai osos allos
english_head: Lead on, then. Speak. Indeed, Socrates, you are just the man to hear it. For the discourse about which we conversed, was in a way, a love-speech. For Lysias has represented one of the beauties being tempted, but not by a lover; this is just the clever thing about it; for he says that favors should be granted rather to the one who is not in love than to the lover. O noble Lysias! I wish he would write that they should be granted to the poor rather than to the rich, to the old rather than to the young, and so of all the other qualities that I and most of us have;

greek_head: ἐμοί τε πρόσεστι καὶ τοῖς πολλοῖς ἡμῶν ἦ γὰρ ἂν ἀστεῖοι καὶ δημωφελεῖς εἶεν οἱ λόγοι ἔγωγ' οὖν οὕτως ἐπιτεθύμηκα
greek_tokens: egw te proseimi kai o polus egw eimi gar an asteios kai dhmwfelhs eien e logos egw oun outws epiqumew akew wste ean badizw poiew o peripatos megarade kai kata hrodikos prosbainw o teixos palin apeimi ou mh su apoleipw pws legw eimi beltistos swkraths oiomai egw o
english_head: for truly his discourse would be witty and of general utility. I am so determined to hear you, that I will not leave you, even if you extend your walk to Megara, and, as Herodicus says, go to the wall and back again. What are you saying, my dear Socrates?

greek_head: Λυσίας ἐν πολλῷ χρόνῳ κατὰ σχολὴν συνέθηκε δεινότατος ὢν τῶν νῦν γράφειν ταῦτα ἰδιώτην ὄντα ἀπομνημονεύσειν
greek_tokens: lusios en polus xronos kata sxolh suntiqhmi deinos eimi o nun grafw outos idiwths eimi apomnhmoneuw acios ekeinos polus ge dew kaitoi boulomai ge an mallon h egw polus xrusion gignomai eimi faidros ei egw faidros agnoew kai emautou epilanqanomai alla gar oudeteros eimi outos eu oida ostis lusios logos akouw ekeinos ou monos apac akouw alla pollakis epanalambanw keleuw e legw o de peiqw
english_head: Do you suppose that I, who am a mere ordinary man, can tell from memory, in a way that is worthy of Lysias, what he, the cleverest writer of our day, composed at his leisure and took a long time for? Far from it; and yet I would rather have that ability than a good sum of money. O Phaedrus! If I don’t know Phaedrus, I have forgotten myself. But since neither of these things is true, I know very well that when listening to Lysias, he did not hear once only, but often urged him to repeat; and he

greek_head: προθύμως τῷ δὲ οὐδὲ ταῦτα ἦν ἱκανά ἀλλὰ τελευτῶν παραλαβὼν τὸ βιβλίον ἃ μάλιστα ἐπεθύμει ἐπεσκόπει καὶ
greek_tokens: proqumos o de oude outos eimi ikanos alla teleutaw paralambanw o biblion o malista epiqumew episkopew kai outos draw ek ewqinos kaqhmai apeipon eis peripatos eimi ws men egw oimh nh o kuwn ecepistamai o logos ei mh panu tis eimi makros eforeuw de ektos teixos ina meletaw apantaw de o nosew peri logos akoh eidon men eidon ezomai ostis exw o sugkorubantiaw
english_head: gladly obeyed. Yet even that was not enough for Phaedrus, but at last he borrowed the book and read what he especially wished, and doing this he sat from early morning. Then, when he grew tired, he went for a walk, with the speech, as I believe, by the Dog, learned by heart, unless it was very long. And he was going outside the wall to practice it. And meeting the man who is sick with the love of discourse, he was glad when he saw him, because he would have someone

greek_head: καὶ προάγειν ἐκέλευε δεομένου δὲ λέγειν τοῦ τῶν λόγων ἐραστοῦ ἐθρύπτετο ὡς δὴ οὐκ ἐπιθυμῶν λέγειν τελευτῶν
greek_tokens: kai proagw keleuw deomai de legw o o logos erasths qruptw ws dh ou epiqumew legw teleutaw de mellw kai ei mh tis ekwn akouw bia erew su oun eimi faidros autos dew osper taxa pantws poihsis nun hdh poiew egw ws alhqhs polus kratistos eimi outws opws dunamai legw os egw dokew su oudamos egw afihmi prin an eipon amos ge pws panu gar su alhqhs dokazw
english_head: to share his revel, and told him to lead on. But when the lover of discourse asked him to speak, he feigned coyness, as if he did not yearn to speak; at last, however, even if no one would listen willingly, he was bound to speak whether or no. So, Phaedrus, ask him to do now what he will presently do anyway. Truly it is best for me to speak as I may; since it is clear that you will not let me go until I speak somehow or other. You have a very correct idea about me.

greek_head: Οὑτωσὶ τοίνυν ποιήσω τῷ ὄντι γάρ ὦ Σώκρατες παντὸς μᾶλλον τά γε ῥήματα οὐκ ἐξέμαθον τὴν
greek_tokens: outwsi toinun poiew o eimi gar eimi swkraths pas mallon o ge rhma ou ekmanqanw o mentoi dianoia sxedon apas os fhmi diaferw o o eraw h o o mh en kefalaios ekastos efechs dieimi arxw apo o proteros deiknumi ge proteros eimi filoths tis ara en o aristeros exis upo o imation topazon gar su exw o logos autos ei de outos eimi outwsi dianoeomai peri egw ws
english_head: Then this is what I will do. Really, Socrates, I have not at all learned the words by heart; but I will repeat the general sense of the whole, the points in which he said the lover was superior to the non-lover, giving them in summary, one after the other, beginning with the first. Yes, my dear, when you have first shown me what you have in your left hand, under your cloak. For I suspect you have the actual discourse. And if that is the case,

greek_head: ἐγώ σε πάνυ μὲν φιλῶ παρόντος δὲ καὶ Λυσίου ἐμαυτόν σοι ἐμμελετᾶν παρέχειν οὐ πάνυ δέδοκται ἀλλ' ἴθι δείκνυε
greek_tokens: egw su panu men filew pareimi de kai lusios emautou su emmeletaw parexw ou panu dokew alla eimi deiknumi pauw ekkrouw egw elpis eimi swkraths os exw en sos ws eggumnazw alla pou dh boulomai kaqizw anagignwskw
english_head: believe this of me, that I am very fond of you, but when Lysias is here I have not the slightest intention of lending you my ears to practice on. Come now, show it. Stop. You have robbed me of the hope I had of practicing on you. But where shall we sit and read?

greek_head: Δεῦρ' ἐκτραπόμενοι κατὰ τὸν Ἰλισὸν ἴωμεν εἶτα ὅπου ἂν δόξῃ ἐν ἡσυχίᾳ καθιζησόμεθα
greek_tokens: deuro ektrepw kata o ilison eimi eita opou an doca en hsuxia kaqizanw eis kairos ws eoika anupodhtos eimi tugxanw su men gar dh aei radios oun egw kata o udation brexw o pous eimi kai ou ahdhs allos te kai ode o oraw o etos te kai o hmeros proagw dh kai skopaw ama opou kaqizanw oraw oun ekeinos o uyhlos platanos tis meis
english_head: Let us turn aside here and go along the Ilissus; then we can sit down quietly wherever we please. I am fortunate, it seems, in being barefoot; you are so always. It is easiest then for us to go along the brook with our feet in the water, and it is not unpleasant, especially at this time of the year and the day. Lead on then, and look out for a good place where we may sit. Do you see that very tall plane tree? What of it?

greek_head: Ἐκεῖ σκιά τ' ἐστὶν καὶ πνεῦμα μέτριον καὶ πόα καθίζεσθαι ἢ ἂν βουλώμεθα κατακλινῆναι
greek_tokens: ekei skia su eimi kai pneuma metrios kai poa kaqizw h an boulomai kataklinw proagw an eipon egw eimi swkraths ou enqende mentoi pinw apo o ilisou legw o boreas o wreiquia arpazw legw gar ara oun enqende xarieis goun kai kaqaros kai diafanhs o udation fainw kai epithdeios korh paizw para autos
english_head: There is shade there and a moderate breeze and grass to sit on, or, if we like, to lie down on. Lead the way. Tell me, Socrates, is it not from some place along here by the Ilissus that Boreas is said to have carried off Oreithyia? Yes, that is the story. Well, is it from here? The streamlet looks very pretty and pure and clear and fit for girls to play by.

greek_head: Οὔκ ἀλλὰ κάτωθεν ὅσον δύ' ἢ τρία στάδια ᾗ πρὸς τὸ ἐν Ἄγρας διαβαίνομεν καί πού τίς ἐστι βωμὸς
greek_tokens: ou alla katwqen osos duh h treis stadion h pros o en agra diabainw kai pou tis eimi bwmos autoqi boreas ou panu noew alla eipon pros zeus eimi swkraths su outos o muqologhma peiqw alhqhs eimi alla ei apistew wsper e sofos ou an atopos eimi eita sofizomai fhmi autos pneuma boreas kata o pimplhmi petra sun farmakeia paizw wqew kai outws dh teleutaw legw upo o boreas anarpastos
english_head: No, the place is about two or three furlongs farther down, where you cross over to the precinct of Agra; and there is an altar of Boreas somewhere thereabouts. I have never noticed it. But, for Heaven’s sake, Socrates, tell me; do you believe this tale is true? If I disbelieved, as the wise men do, I should not be extraordinary; then I might give a rational explanation, that a blast of Boreas, the north wind, pushed her off the neighboring rocks as she was playing with Pharmacea, and

greek_head: γεγονέναι ἢ ἐξ Ἀρείου πάγου λέγεται γὰρ αὖ καὶ οὗτος ὁ λόγος ὡς ἐκεῖθεν ἀλλ' οὐκ ἐνθένδε ἡρπάσθη ἐγὼ δέ
greek_tokens: gignomai h ek areios pagos legw gar au kai outos o logos ws ekeiqen alla ou enqende arpazw egw de eimi faidros allos men o toioutos xarieis hgeomai lian de deinos kai epiponos kai ou panu eutuxhs anhr kata allos men oudeis ostis de autos anagkh meta outos o o ippokentauros eidos epanorqow kai auqis o o ximaira kai epirrew de oxlos toioutos gorgwn kai phgazw kai
english_head: that when she had died in this manner she was said to have been carried off by Boreas. But I, Phaedrus, think such explanations are very pretty in general, but are the inventions of a very clever and laborious and not altogether enviable man, for no other reason than because after this he must explain the forms of the Centaurs, and then that of the Chimaera, and there presses in upon him a whole crowd of such creatures, Gorgons and Pegas, and multitudes

greek_head: ἄλλων ἀμηχάνων πλήθη τε καὶ ἀτοπίαι τερατολόγων τινῶν φύσεων αἷς εἴ τις ἀπιστῶν προσβιβᾷ κατὰ τὸ εἰκὸς ἕκαστον
greek_tokens: allos amhxanos pimplhmi te kai atopia teratologos tis fusis os ei tis apistew prosbibazw kata o eoika ekastos ate agroikos tis sofia xraomai polus autos sxolazw dehsis egw de pros autos oudamos eimi sxolh o de aitios eimi filos outos ode ou dunamai pw kata o delfikos gramma gignwskw emautou geloios dh egw fainw
english_head: of strange, inconceivable, portentous natures. If anyone disbelieves in these, and with a rustic sort of wisdom, undertakes to explain each in accordance with probability, he will need a great deal of leisure. But I have no leisure for them at all; and the reason, my friend, is this: I am not yet able, as the Delphic inscription has it, to know myself; so it seems to me ridiculous,

greek_head: τοῦτο ἔτι ἀγνοοῦντα τὰ ἀλλότρια σκοπεῖν ὅθεν δὴ χαίρειν ἐάσας ταῦτα πειθόμενος δὲ τῷ νομιζομένῳ περὶ αὐτῶν ὃ
greek_tokens: outos eti agnoew o allotrios skopaw oqen dh xairw eaw outos peiqw de o nomizw peri autos o nundi elegos skopaw ou outos alla emautou eite tis qhraw eimi tugxanw tufwn poluplokos kai mallon epitufomai eite hmeros te kai aploos zwion qeia tis kai atufos moira fusis metexw atar eimi etairos metacu o logos ara ou ode eimi o dendron epi osper agw egw
english_head: when I do not yet know that, to investigate irrelevant things. And so I dismiss these matters and accepting the customary belief about them, as I was saying just now, I investigate not these things, but myself, to know whether I am a monster more complicated and more furious than Typhon or a gentler and simpler creature, to whom a divine and quiet lot is given by nature. But, my friend, while we were talking, is not this the tree to which you were leading us?

greek_head: Τοῦτο μὲν οὖν αὐτό Νὴ τὴν Ἥραν καλή γε ἡ καταγωγή ἥ τε γὰρ
greek_tokens: outos men oun autos nh o hra kalos ge o katagwgh o te gar platanos outos mala amfilafhs te kai uyhlos o te agnos o uyos kai o suskios pagkalos kai ws akmh exis o anqh ws an euwdhs parexw o topos o te au phgh xarieis upo o platanos reomai mala yuxroomai udwr wste ge o podis tekmairomai numfh su tis kai axelwios ieron apo o korh te kai agalma eoika eimi
english_head: Yes, this is it. By Hera, it is a charming resting place. For this plane tree is very spreading and lofty, and the tall and shady willow is very beautiful, and it is in full bloom, so as to make the place most fragrant; then, too, the spring is very pretty as it flows under the plane tree, and its water is very cool, to judge by my foot. And it seems to be a sacred place of some nymphs and of Achelous, judging by

greek_head: εἰ δ' αὖ βούλει τὸ εὔπνουν τοῦ τόπου ὡς ἀγαπητὸν καὶ σφόδρα ἡδύ θερινόν τε καὶ λιγυρὸν ὑπηχεῖ τῷ τῶν τεττίγων
greek_tokens: ei de au boulomai o eupnous o topos ws agaphtos kai sfodra hdus qerinos te kai liguros uphxew o o tettic xoros pas de komyos o o poa ostis en hrema prosanths ikanos fuw kataklinw o kefalh pagkalos exw wste ariston su cenagew eimi filos faidros su de ge eimi qaumasios atopos tis fainw atexnos gar o legw cenagew tis kai ou epixwrios
english_head: the figurines and statues. Then again, if you please, how lovely and perfectly charming the breeziness of the place is! and it resounds with the shrill summer music of the chorus of cicadas. But the most delightful thing of all is the grass, as it grows on the gentle slope, thick enough to be just right when you lay your head on it. So you have guided the stranger most excellently, dear Phaedrus. You are an amazing and most remarkable person. For you really do seem exactly like a stranger who is being guided about,

greek_head: ἔοικας οὕτως ἐκ τοῦ ἄστεος οὔτ' εἰς τὴν ὑπερορίαν ἀποδημεῖς οὔτ' ἔξω τείχους ἔμοιγε δοκεῖς τὸ παράπαν ἐξιέναι
greek_tokens: eoika outws ek o astu oute eis o uperorios apodhmew oute ecw teixos egw dokew o parapan eceimi suggignwskw egw eimi aristos filomaqhs gar eimi o men oun xwrion kai o dendron oudeis egw eqelw didaskw e de en o astu anqrwpos su mentoi dokew egw o emew ecodos o farmakon euriskw wsper gar e o peinaw qremma qallos h tis karpos proseaw agw su egw logos outws proteinw en biblion o te
english_head: and not like a native. You don’t go away from the city out over the border, and it seems to me you don’t go outside the walls at all. Forgive me, my dear friend. You see, I am fond of learning. Now the country places and the trees won’t teach me anything, and the people in the city do. But you seem to have found the charm to bring me out. For as people lead hungry animals by shaking in front of them a branch of leaves or some fruit, just so, I think, you, by holding before me discourses in books,

greek_head: Ἀττικὴν φαίνῃ περιάξειν ἅπασαν καὶ ὅποι ἂν ἄλλοσε βούλῃ νῦν δ' οὖν ἐν τῷ παρόντι δεῦρ' ἀφικόμενος ἐγὼ μέν μοι
greek_tokens: attikos fainw periagnumi apas kai opoi an allote boulomai nun de oun en o pareimi deuro afikneomai egw men egw dokazw katakeimai su de en opoios sxhma oiomai radios anagignwskw outos airew anagignwskw akouw dh peri men o emew pragma efisthmi kai ws nomizw sumferw egw gignomai outos akouw aciaw de mh dia
english_head: will lead me all over Attica and wherever else you please. So now that I have come here, I intend to lie down, and do you choose the position in which you think you can read most easily, and read. Hear then. You know what my condition is, and you have heard how I think it is to our advantage to arrange these matters.

greek_head: τοῦτο ἀτυχῆσαι ὧν δέομαι ὅτι οὐκ ἐραστὴς ὤν σου τυγχάνω ὡς ἐκείνοις μὲν τότε μεταμέλει ὧν ἂν εὖ ποιήσωσιν
greek_tokens: outos atuxew os deomai ostis ou erasths eimi su tugxanw ws ekeinos men tote metamelomai os an eu poiew epeidan o epiqumia pauw o de ou eimi xronos en os metagignwskw proshkw ou gar upo anagkh alla ekwn ws an ariston peri o oikew bouleuw pros o dunamis o eautou eu poiew eti de e men eraw skopaw o te kakos diatiqhmi o eautou dia o erws kai o poiew eu kai os exw ponos prostiqhmi
english_head: And I claim that I ought not to be refused what I ask because I am not your lover. For lovers repent of the kindnesses they have done when their passion ceases; but there is no time when non-lovers naturally repent. For they do kindnesses to the best of their ability, not under compulsion, but of their free will, according to their view of their own best interest. And besides, lovers consider the injury they have done to their own concerns on account of their love, and the benefits they have conferred, and they add the trouble they have had,

greek_head: ἡγοῦνται πάλαι τὴν ἀξίαν ἀποδεδωκέναι χάριν τοῖς ἐρωμένοις τοῖς δὲ μὴ ἐρῶσιν οὔτε τὴν τῶν οἰκείων ἀμέλειαν
greek_tokens: hgeomai pala o acios apodidwmi xaris o eraomai o de mh eraw oute o o oikew ameleia dia outos eimi profasizomai oute o parerxomai ponos upologizomai oute o pros o proshkw diafora aitiaomai wste periairew tosoutos kakh oudeis upoleipw alla h poiew proqumos ostis an autos oiomai prassw xarizomai eti de ei dia outos acios
english_head: and so they think they have long ago made sufficient return to the beloved; but non-lovers cannot ever neglect of their own affairs because of their condition, nor can they take account of the pains they have been at in the past, nor lay any blame for quarrels with their relatives; and so, since all these evils are removed, there is nothing left for them but to do eagerly what they think will please the beloved.

greek_head: τοὺς ἐρῶντας περὶ πολλοῦ ποιεῖσθαι ὅτι τούτους μάλιστά φασιν φιλεῖν ὧν ἂν ἐρῶσιν καὶ ἕτοιμοί εἰσι καὶ ἐκ τῶν λόγων
greek_tokens: o eraw peri polus poiew ostis outos malista fhmi filew os an eraw kai etoimos eimi kai ek o logos kai ek o ergnumi o allos apexqanomai o eraomai xarizomai radios gignwskw ei alhqhs legw ostis osos an usteron eramai ekeinos autos peri pleiwn poiew kai dhlos ostis ean ekeinos dokazw kai outos kakos poiew kaitoi pws eoika eimi toioutos pragma proihmi
english_head: And besides, if lovers ought to be highly esteemed because they say they have the greatest love for the objects of their passion, since both by word and deed they are ready to make themselves hated by others to please the beloved, it is easy to see that, if what they say is true, whenever they fall in love afterwards, they will care for the new love more than for the old and will certainly injure the old love, if that pleases the new. And how can one reasonably entrust matters of such importance to one who is afflicted with a disease

greek_head: τοιαύτην ἔχοντι συμφοράν ἣν οὐδ' ἂν ἐπιχειρήσειεν οὐδεὶς ἔμπειρος ὢν ἀποτρέπειν καὶ γὰρ αὐτοὶ ὁμολογοῦσι νοσεῖν
greek_tokens: toioutos exw sumfora os oude an epixeirew oudeis empeiros eimi apotrepw kai gar autos omologew nosew mallon h swfronew kai oida ostis kakos fronew alla ou dunamai eautou kratew wste pws an eu fronew outos kalos exw hgeomai peri os outws diakeimai bouleuw kai men dh ei men ek o eraw o beltistos airew ek oligos an su o eklecis eaw ei de ek o allos o sautou epithdeios ek polus wste polus
english_head: such that no one of any experience would even try to cure it? For they themselves confess that they are insane, rather than in their right mind, and that they know they are foolish, but cannot control themselves; and so, how could they, when they have come to their senses, think those acts were good which they determined upon when in such a condition? And if you were to choose the best from among your lovers, your choice would be limited to a few; whereas it would be made from a great number, if you chose the most congenial from non-lovers,

greek_head: πλείων ἐλπὶς ἐν τοῖς πολλοῖς ὄντα τυχεῖν τὸν ἄξιον τῆς σῆς φιλίας
greek_tokens: plew elpis en o polus eimi tugxanw o acios o sew filios ei toinun o nomos o kaqisthmi deidw mh puqw o anqrwpos oneidos su gignomai eoika eimi
english_head: so that you would have a better chance, in choosing among many, of finding the one most worthy of your affection. Now if you are afraid of public opinion, and fear that if people find out your love affair you will be disgraced,

greek_head: τοὺς μὲν ἐρῶντας οὕτως ἂν οἰομένους καὶ ὑπὸ τῶν ἄλλων ζηλοῦσθαι ὥσπερ αὐτοὺς ὑφ' αὑτῶν ἐπαρθῆναι τῷ λέγειν
greek_tokens: o men eraw outws an oiomai kai upo o allos zhlow wsper autos ufh eautou epairw o legw kai filotimeomai epideiknumi pros apas ostis ou allos autos poneomai o de mh eraw kreisswn eautou eimi o beltistos anti o doca o para o anqrwpos airew eti de o men eraw polus anagkh punqanomai kai eidon akolouqew o eraomai kai ergnumi outos poiew wste otan oraw dialegw
english_head: consider that lovers, believing that others would be as envious of them as they are of others, are likely to be excited by possession and in their pride to show everybody that they have not toiled in vain; but the non-lovers, since they have control of their feelings, are likely to choose what is really best, rather than to court the opinion of mankind. Moreover, many are sure to notice and see the lovers going about with their beloved ones and making that

greek_head: ἀλλήλοις τότε αὐτοὺς οἴονται ἢ γεγενημένης ἢ μελλούσης ἔσεσθαι τῆς ἐπιθυμίας συνεῖναι τοὺς δὲ μὴ ἐρῶντας
greek_tokens: allhlwn tote autos oiomai h gignomai h mellw eimi o epiqumia suneimi o de mh eraw oude aitiaomai dia o sunousia epixeirew oida ostis anagkaion eimi h dia filios tis dialegw h dia allos tis hdona kai men dh ei su deos paristhmi hgeomai xalepos eimi filios summenw kai allos men tropos diafora gignomai koinos an amfoteros kaqisthmi o
english_head: their chief business, and so, when they are seen talking with each other, people think they are met in connection with some love-matter either past or future; but no one ever thinks of finding fault with non-lovers because they meet, since everyone knows that one must converse with somebody, either because of friendship or because it is pleasant for some other reason. And then, too, if you are frightened by the thought that it is hard for friendship to last, and that under other circumstances any quarrel would be an equal misfortune to both, but that when you have surrendered

greek_head: συμφοράν προεμένου δέ σου ἃ περὶ πλείστου ποιῇ μεγάλην ἄν σοι βλάβην ἂν γενέσθαι εἰκότως ἂν τοὺς ἐρῶντας μᾶλλον
greek_tokens: sumfora proihmi de su o peri pleistos poiew megas an su blabh an gignomai eoikotws an o eraw mallon an fobew polus gar autos eimi o lupew kai panth epi o eautou blabh nomizw gignomai dioper kai o pros o allos o eraomai sunousia apotrepw fobew o men ousia ktaomai mh xrhma autos uperballw o de paideuw mh suneimi kreisswn gignomai o de allos tis ktaomai
english_head: what you prize most highly you would be the chief sufferer, it would be reasonable for you to be more afraid of the lovers; for they are pained by many things and they think everything that happens is done for the sake of hurting them. Therefore they prevent their loves from associating with other men, for they fear the wealthy, lest their money give them an advantage, and the educated, lest they prove superior in intellect; and they are on their guard

greek_head: ἀγαθὸν τὴν δύναμιν ἑκάστου φυλάττονται πείσαντες μὲν οὖν ἀπεχθέσθαι σε τούτοις εἰς ἐρημίαν φίλων καθιστᾶσιν
greek_tokens: agaqos o dunamis ekastos fulassw peiqw men oun apexqanomai su outos eis erhmia filos kaqisthmi ean de o sautou skopaw ameinwn ekeinos fronew hkw autos eis diafora osos de mh eraw tugxanw alla dia areth prassw os deomai ou an o suneimi fqonew alla o mh eqelw misew hgeomai upo ekeinos men uperoraw upo o suneimi de wfelew wste polus
english_head: against the influence of everyone who possesses any other good thing. If now they persuade you to incur the dislike of all these, they involve you in a dearth of friends, and if you consider your own interest and are more sensible than they, you will have to quarrel with them. But those who are not in love, but who have gained the satisfaction of their desires because of their merit, would not be jealous of those who associated with you, but would hate those who did not wish to do so, thinking that you are slighted by these last and benefited by the former,

greek_head: πλείων ἐλπὶς φιλίαν αὐτοῖς ἐκ τοῦ πράγματος ἢ ἔχθραν γενέσθαι
greek_tokens: plew elpis filios autos ek o pragma h exqra gignomai kai men dh o men eraw polus proteros o swma epiqumew h o tropos gignwskw kai o allos oikew empeiros gignomai wste adhlos autos ei eti tote boulomai filos eimi epeidan o epiqumia pauw
english_head: so that there is much more likelihood that they will gain friendship than enmity from their love-affair with you. And then, too, many lovers are moved by physical passion before they know the character or have become acquainted with the connections of the beloved, so that it is uncertain whether they will wish to be your friends

greek_head: τοῖς δὲ μὴ ἐρῶσιν οἳ καὶ πρότερον ἀλλήλοις φίλοι ὄντες ταῦτα ἔπραξαν οὐκ ἐξ ὧν ἂν εὖ πάθωσι ταῦτα εἰκὸς ἐλάττω
greek_tokens: o de mh eraw e kai proteros allhlwn filos eimi outos prassw ou ek os an eu pasxw outos eoika elasswn o filios autos poiew alla outos mnhmeion katalimpanw o mellw eimi kai men dh beltiwn su proshkw gignomai egw peiqw h erasths ekeinos men gar kai para o beltistos o te legw kai o prassw epainew o men deidw mh apexqomai o de
english_head: after their passion has ceased. But in the case of those who are not in love, who were your friends before entering into the closer relation, the favors received are not likely to make the friendship less, but will remain as pledges of future joys. And then, too, it will be better for your character to yield to me than to a lover. For lovers praise your words and acts beyond due measure, partly through fear of incurring your displeasure,

greek_head: καὶ αὐτοὶ χεῖρον διὰ τὴν ἐπιθυμίαν γιγνώσκοντες τοιαῦτα γὰρ ὁ ἔρως ἐπιδείκνυται δυστυχοῦντας μέν ἃ μὴ λύπην
greek_tokens: kai autos xeirwn dia o epiqumia gignwskw toioutos gar o eros epideiknumi dustuxew men o mh luph o allos paraxew aniaros poiew nomizw eutuxew de kai o mh hdona acios para ekeinos epainos anagkazw tugxanw wste polus mallon eleaw o eraomai h zhlew autos proshkw ean de egw peiqw proteros men ou o pareimi hdona qerapeuw suneimi su alla kai
english_head: and partly because their own judgment is obscured by their passion. For such are the exhibitions of the power of Love: he makes the unsuccessful lovers think that things are grievous which cause no pain to others, and he compels the successful to praise what ought not to give pleasure; therefore those whom they love are more to be pitied than envied. But if you yield to me, I shall consort with you, not with a view to present pleasure only, but to

greek_head: τὴν μέλλουσαν ὠφελίαν ἔσεσθαι οὐχ ὑπ' ἔρωτος ἡττώμενος ἀλλ' ἐμαυτοῦ κρατῶν οὐδὲ διὰ σμικρὰ ἰσχυρὰν ἔχθραν ἀναιρούμενος
greek_tokens: o mellw wfeleia eimi ou upo erws hssaomai alla emautou kras oude dia mikros isxuros exqra anairew alla dia megas bradus oligos orgh poiew o men akousis suggnwmh exw o de ekousios peiraw apotrepw outos gar eimi filios polus xronos eimi tekmhrion ei de ara su outos paristhmi ws ou oios te isxuros filios gignomai ean mh tis era tugxanw
english_head: future advantage also, not being overcome by passion but in full control of myself, and not taking up violent enmity because of small matters, but slowly gathering little anger when the transgressions are great, forgiving involuntary wrongs and trying to prevent intentional ones; for these are the proofs of a friendship that will endure for a long time. But if you have a notion that friendship cannot be firm,

greek_head: ἐνθυμεῖσθαι χρὴ ὅτι οὔτ' ἂν τοὺς ὑεῖς περὶ πολλοῦ ἐποιούμεθα οὔτ' ἂν τοὺς πατέρας καὶ τὰς μητέρας οὔτ' ἂν πιστοὺς
greek_tokens: enqumeomai xrh ostis oute an o uios peri polus epoizw oute an o pathr kai o mhthr oute an pistos filos ktaomai e ou ek epiqumia toioutos gignomai alla ek eteros epithdeuma eti de ei xrh o deomai malista xarizomai proshkw kai o allos mh o beltistos alla o aporos eu poiew megas gar apallassw kakh pleistos xaris autos eimi kai men dh kai en o
english_head: unless one is in love, you should bear in mind that in that case we should not have great affection for sons or for fathers and mothers, nor should we possess faithful friends who have been gained not through passion but through associations of a different kind. Besides, if you ought to grant favors to those who ask for them most eagerly, you ought in other matters also to confer benefits, not on the best, but on the most needy; for they will be most grateful, since they are relieved of the greatest ills. And then, too,

greek_head: ἰδίαις δαπάναις οὐ τοὺς φίλους ἄξιον παρακαλεῖν ἀλλὰ τοὺς προσαιτοῦντας καὶ τοὺς δεομένους πλησμονῆς ἐκεῖνοι
greek_tokens: idios dapanh ou o filos acios parakalew alla o prosaitew kai o deomai plhsmonh ekeinos gar kai agapaw kai akolouqew kai epi o qura hkw kai malista hdomai kai ou elaxistos xaris eimi kai polus agaqos autos euxomai alla isos proshkw ou o sfodra deomai xarizomai alla o malista apodidwmi xaris dunamai oude o prosaitew
english_head: at private entertainments you ought not to invite your friends, but beggars and those who need a meal; for they will love you and attend you and come to your doors and be most pleased and grateful, and will call down many blessings upon your head. Perhaps, however, you ought not to grant favors to those who beg for them, but to those who are most able to repay you; and not to those who ask merely, but to the most deserving; and not to those

greek_head: μόνον ἀλλὰ τοῖς τοῦ πράγματος ἀξίοις οὐδὲ ὅσοι τῆς σῆς ὥρας ἀπολαύσονται ἀλλ' οἵτινες πρεσβυτέρῳ γενομένῳ τῶν
greek_tokens: monos alla o o pragma acios oude osos o sew oraw apolauw alla ostis presbus gignomai o sfeteros agaqos metadidwmi oude e diaprassw pros o allos filotimeomai alla ostis aisxunw pros apas siwpaw oude o oligos xronos spoudazw alla o omoios dia pas o bios filos eimi oude ostis pauw o epiqumia exqra profasis zhtew alla e pauw o oraw tote
english_head: who will enjoy your youthful beauty, but to those who will share their good things with you when you are older; and not to those who, when they have succeeded, will boast to others of their success, but to those who will modestly keep it a secret from all; and not to those who will be enamored for a little while, but to those who will be your friends for life; and not to those who will seek a pretext for a quarrel when their passion has died out, but

greek_head: τὴν αὑτῶν ἀρετὴν ἐπιδείξονται σὺ οὖν τῶν τε εἰρημένων μέμνησο καὶ ἐκεῖνο ἐνθυμοῦ ὅτι τοὺς μὲν ἐρῶντας οἱ φίλοι
greek_tokens: o eautou areth epideiknumi su oun o te erw mimnhskw kai ekeinos enqumeomai ostis o men eraw e filos nouqetew ws eimi kakos o epithdeuma o de mh eraw oudeis pwpote o oikew memfomai ws dia outos kakos bouleuw peri eautou isos an oun eromai egw ei apas su parainew o mh eraw xarizomai egw men oimh oude an o eraw pros apas su keleuw o eraw outos exw o
english_head: to those who will show their own merit when your youth is passed. Do you, then, remember what I have said, and bear this also in mind, that lovers are admonished by their friends, who think their way of life is bad, but no relative ever blamed a non-lover for bad management of his own interests on account of that condition. Perhaps you may ask me if I advise you to grant favors to all non-lovers. But I think the lover would not urge you to be so disposed

greek_head: διάνοιαν οὔτε γὰρ τῷ λαμβάνοντι χάριτος ἴσης ἄξιον οὔτε σοὶ βουλομένῳ τοὺς ἄλλους λανθάνειν ὁμοίως δυνατόν
greek_tokens: dianoia oute gar o lambanw xaris isos acios oute sos boulomai o allos lanqanw omoios dunatos deomai de blabh men apo autos mhdeis wfeleia de amfw gignomai egw men oun ikanos egw nomizw o erw ei de eti tis su poqew hgeomai paraleipw erwtaw tis su fainw eimi swkraths o logos ou uperfuhs o te allos kai o onoma erw
english_head: toward all lovers either; for the favor, if scattered broadcast, is not so highly prized by the rational recipient, nor can you, if you wish, keep your relations with one hidden from the rest. But from love no harm ought to come, but benefit to both parties. Now I think I have said enough. But if you feel any lack, or think anything has been omitted, ask questions. What do you think of the discourse, Socrates?

greek_head: Δαιμονίως μὲν οὖν ὦ ἑταῖρε ὥστε με ἐκπλαγῆναι καὶ τοῦτο ἐγὼ ἔπαθον διὰ σέ ὦ Φαῖδρε πρὸς σὲ ἀποβλέπων
greek_tokens: daimonios men oun eimi etairos wste egw ekplassw kai outos egw pasxw dia sos eimi faidros pros sos apoblepw ostis egw dokew ganumai upo o logos metacu anagignwskw hgeomai gar sos mallon h egw epaiw peri o toioutos sos epomai kai epomai sunbakxeuw meta sew o qeia kefalh eien outws dh dokew paizw dokazw gar su paizw kai ou spoudazw
english_head: Is it not wonderful, especially in diction? More than that, it is miraculous, my friend; I am quite overcome by it. And this is due to you, Phaedrus, because as I looked at you, I saw that you were delighted by the speech as you read. So, thinking that you know more than I about such matters, I followed in your train and joined you in the divine frenzy. Indeed! So you see fit to make fun of it? Do I seem to you to be joking and not to be in earnest?

greek_head: Μηδαμῶς ὦ Σώκρατες ἀλλ' ὡς ἀληθῶς εἰπὲ πρὸς Διὸς φιλίου οἴει ἄν τινα ἔχειν εἰπεῖν ἄλλον τῶν
greek_tokens: mhdamos eimi swkraths alla ws alhqhs eipon pros zeus filios oiomai an tis exw eipon allos o ellhn eteros outos megas kai plew peri o autos pragma tis de kai outos deomai upo egw te kai sew o logos epainew ws o deon erw o poihths alla ou ekeinos monos ostis safhs kai stroggulos kai akribhs ekastos o onoma apotorneuw ei gar deomai sugxwrhteon xaris shs epei egw ge elaunw upo o emew
english_head: Do not jest, Socrates, but, in the name of Zeus, the god of friendship, tell me truly, do you think any other of the Greeks could speak better or more copiously than this on the same subject? What? Are you and I to praise the discourse because the author has said what he ought, and not merely because all the expressions are clear and well rounded and finely turned? For if that is expected, I must grant it for your sake, since, because of my stupidity, I did not notice it.

greek_head: οὐδενίας τῷ γὰρ ῥητορικῷ αὐτοῦ μόνῳ τὸν νοῦν προςεῖχον τοῦτο δὲ οὐδ' ἂν αὐτὸν ᾤμην Λυσίαν οἴεσθαι ἱκανὸν
greek_tokens: oudenia o gar rhtorikos autos monos o new prosexw outos de oude an autos oiomai lusios oiomai ikanos eimi kai oun egw dokew eimi faidros ei mh tis su allos legw dis kai tris o autos erw ws ou panu euporew o polus legw peri o autos h isos oudeis autos melw o toioutos kai fainw dh egw neanieuomai epideiknumi ws oios te eimi tautos eteros te kai eteros legos amfoteros eipon ariston
english_head: I was attending only to the rhetorical manner, and I thought even Lysias himself would not think that satisfactory. It seemed to me, Phaedrus, unless you disagree, that he said the same thing two or three times, as if he did not find it easy to say many things about one subject, or perhaps he did not care about such a detail; and he appeared to me in youthful fashion to be exhibiting his ability to say the same thing in two different ways and in both ways excellently.

greek_head: Οὐδὲν λέγεις ὦ Σώκρατες αὐτὸ γὰρ τοῦτο καὶ μάλιστα ὁ λόγος ἔχει τῶν γὰρ ἐνόντων ἀξίως ῥηθῆναι
greek_tokens: oudeis legw eimi swkraths autos gar outos kai malista o logos exis o gar eneimi acios erw en o pragma oudeis paraleipw wste para o ekeinos erw mhdeis an pote dunamai eipon allos plew kai pleiwn acios outos egw su ouketi oios su eimi peiqw palaios gar kai sofos anhr te kai gunh peri autos erw kai grafw ecelegxw egw ean su xarizomai sugxwrew
english_head: Nonsense, Socrates! Why that is the especial merit of the discourse. He has omitted none of the points that belong to the subject, so that nobody could ever speak about it more exhaustively or worthily than he has done. There I must cease to agree with you; for the wise men and women of old, who have spoken and written about these matters, will rise up to confute me, if, to please you, I assent.

greek_head: Τίνες οὗτοι καὶ ποῦ σὺ βελτίω τούτων ἀκήκοας Νῦν μὲν οὕτως οὐκ ἔχω εἰπεῖν δῆλον δὲ ὅτι τινῶν
greek_tokens: tinw outos kai pou su beltiwn outos akouw nun men outws ou exw eipon dhlos de ostis tis akouw h pou sapfw o kalew h anakrewn o sofos h kai suggrafeus tis pinw dh tekmairomai legos plhrhs pws eimi daimonios o sthqos exw aisqanomai para outos an exw eipon eteros mh xeirwn ostis men oun para ge emautou oudeis autos ennoew eu oida sunoida emautou amaqia leipw dh oimh ek allotrios pinw
english_head: Who are they? and where have you heard anything better than this? I cannot say, just at this moment; but I certainly must have heard something, either from the lovely Sappho or the wise Anacreon, or perhaps from some prose writers. What ground have I for saying so? Why, my dear friend, I feel that my own bosom is full, and that I could make another speech, different from this and quite as good. Now I am conscious of my own ignorance, and I know very well that I have never invented these things myself, so the only alternative

greek_head: ναμάτων διὰ τῆς ἀκοῆς πεπληρῶσθαί με δίκην ἀγγείου ὑπὸ δὲ νωθείας αὖ καὶ αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἐπιλέλησμαι ὅπως τε
greek_tokens: nama dia o akoh plhrow egw dikh aggeion upo de nwqeia au kai autos outos epilanqanomai opws te kai ostis akouw alla eimi gennaios kallista erw su gar egw ostis men kai opws akouw mhde an keleuw eipon outos de autos o legw poiew o en o biblion beltiwn te kai mh elasswn eteros upisxneomai eipon outos apexw kai su egw wsper e ennea arxw upisxneomai xruseos eikwn isometrhtos eis delfoi anaqew ou
english_head: is that I have been filled through the ears, like a pitcher, from the well springs of another; but, again because of my stupidity, I have forgotten how and from whom I heard it. Most noble Socrates, that is splendid! Don’t tell, even if I beg you, how or from whom you heard it; only do as you say; promise to make another speech better than that in the book and no shorter and quite different. Then I promise, like the nine archons, to set up at Delphi a statue as large as life,

greek_head: μόνον ἐμαυτοῦ ἀλλὰ καὶ σήν Φίλτατος εἶ καὶ ὡς ἀληθῶς χρυσοῦς ὦ Φαῖδρε
greek_tokens: monos emautou alla kai shs filtatos ei kai ws alhqhs xruseos eimi faidros ei egw oiomai legw ws lusios o pas amartanw kai oios te dh para pas outos allos eipon outos de oimh oude an o faulos pasxw suggrafeus autika peri e o logos tis oiomai legw ws xrh mh eraw mallon h eraw xarizomai parihmi o men o fronimos egkwmiazw
english_head: not only of myself, but of you also. You are a darling and truly golden, Phaedrus, if you think I mean that Lysias has failed in every respect and that I can compose a discourse containing nothing that he has said. That, I fancy, could not happen even to the worst writer. For example, to take the subject of his speech, who do you suppose, in arguing that the non-lover ought to be more favored than the lover,

greek_head: τοῦ δὲ τὸ ἄφρον ψέγειν ἀναγκαῖα γοῦν ὄντα εἶτ' ἄλλ' ἄττα ἕξειν λέγειν ἀλλ' οἶμαι τὰ μὲν τοιαῦτα ἐατέα
greek_tokens: o de o afrwn yegw anagkaion goun eimi eimi allos atta exw legw alla oimh o men toioutos eateos kai suggnwsteos legw kai o men toioutos ou o euresis alla o diaqesis epaineteon o de mh anagkaion te kai xalepos euriskw pros o diaqesis kai o euresis sugxwrew o legw metrios gar egw dokew erw poiew oun kai egw outws o men o eraw
english_head: could omit praise of the non-lover’s calm sense and blame of the lover’s unreason, which are inevitable arguments, and then say something else instead? No, such arguments, I think, must be allowed and excused; and in these the arrangement, not the invention, is to be praised; but in the case of arguments which are not inevitable and are hard to discover, the invention deserves praise as well as the arrangement. I concede your point, for I think what you say is reasonable, So I will make this concession:

greek_head: τοῦ μὴ ἐρῶντος μᾶλλον νοσεῖν δώσω σοι ὑποτίθεσθαι τῶν δὲ λοιπῶν ἕτερα πλείω καὶ πλείονος ἄξια εἰπὼν τῶνδε
greek_tokens: o mh eraw mallon nosew didwmi su upotiqhmi o de loipazw eteros plew kai pleiwn acios eipon ode lusios para o kuyelidai anaqhma sfurhlatos en olumpias isthmi spoudazw eimi faidros ostis su o paidikon epilambanw eresxhlew su kai oiomai dh egw ws alhqhs epixeirew eipon para o ekeinos sofia eteros tis poikilos peri men outos eimi filos eis o omoios labh
english_head: I will allow you to begin with the premise that the lover is more distraught than the non-lover; and if you speak on the remaining points more copiously and better than Lysias, without saying the same things, your statue of beaten metal shall stand at Olympia beside the offering of the Cypselids. Have you taken my jest in earnest, Phaedrus, because, to tease you, I laid hands on your beloved, and do you really suppose I am going to try to surpass the rhetoric of Lysias and make a speech more ingenious than his? Now, my friend, you have given me

greek_head: ἐλήλυθας ῥητέον μὲν γάρ σοι παντὸς μᾶλλον οὕτως ὅπως οἷός τε εἶ ἵνα μὴ τὸ τῶν κωμῳδῶν φορτικὸν πρᾶγμα
greek_tokens: erxomai rhteos men gar su pas mallon outws opws oios te ei ina mh o o kwmwdew fortikos pragma anagkazw poiew antapodidwmi allhlwn eulabeomai kai mh boulomai egw anagkazw legw ekeinos o ei egw eimi swkraths swkraths agnoew kai emautou epilanqanomai kai ostis epequme men legw qruptw de alla dianoeomai ostis enteuqen ou apeimi prin an su eipon o fhmi en o sthqos exw eimi de monos en erhmia
english_head: a fair hold; for you certainly must speak as best you can, lest we be compelled to resort to the comic you’re another; be careful and do not force me to say O Socrates, if I don’t know Socrates, I have forgotten myself, and he yearned to speak, but feigned coyness. Just make up your mind that we are not going away from here until you speak out what you said you had in your breast. We are alone

greek_head: ἰσχυρότερος δ' ἐγὼ καὶ νεώτερος ἐκ δὲ ἁπάντων τούτων σύνες ὅ τοι λέγω καὶ μηδαμῶς πρὸς βίαν βουληθῇς
greek_tokens: isxuros de egw kai neos ek de apas outos sunihmi o su legos kai mhdamos pros bia boulomai mallon h ekwn legw alla eimi makarios faidros geloios eimi para agaqos poihths idiwths autosxediazw peri o autos oistos ws exis pauw pros egw kallwpizw sxedon gar exw o eipon anagkazw su legw mhdamos toinun eipon ou alla kai dh legos o de egw logos orkos eimi omnumi gar su tis mentoi tis qea h boulomai
english_head: in a solitary spot, and I am stronger and younger than you; so, under these circumstances, take my meaning, and speak voluntarily, rather than under compulsion. But, my dear Phaedrus, I shall make myself ridiculous if I, a mere amateur, try without preparation to speak on the same subject in competition with a master of his art. Now listen to me. Stop trying to fool me; for I can say something which will force you to speak. Then pray don’t say it. Yes, but I will. And my saying shall be an oath. I swear to you by—

greek_head: τὴν πλάτανον ταυτηνί ἦ μήν ἐάν μοι μὴ εἴπῃς τὸν λόγον ἐναντίον αὐτῆς ταύτης μηδέποτέ σοι ἕτερον λόγον
greek_tokens: o platanos outos eimi meis ean egw mh eipon o logos enantion autos outos mhdepote su eteros logos mhdeis mhdeis mhte epideiknumi mhte ecaggellw babai eimi miaros ws eu aneuriskw o anagkh andris filologos poiew o an keleuw tis dhta exw strefw oudeis eti epei su ge outos omnumi pws gar an oios su eimi toioutos qoinh apexw
english_head: by what god? By this plane tree? I take my solemn oath that unless you produce the discourse in the very presence of this plane tree, I will never read you another or tell you of another. Oh! Oh! You wretch! How well you found out how to make a lover of discourse do your will! Then why do you try to get out of it? I won’t any more, since you have taken this oath; for how could I give up such pleasures?

greek_head: Λέγε δή Οἶσθ' οὖν ὡς ποιήσω

Now he who is not newly initiated, or has been corrupted, does not quickly rise from this world to that other world and to absolute beauty when he sees its namesake here, and so he does not revere it when he looks upon it, but gives himself up to pleasure and like a beast proceeds to lust and begetting;

But he who is newly initiated, who beheld many of those realities, when he sees a godlike face or form which is a good image of beauty, shudders at first, and something of the old awe comes over him, then, as he gazes, he reveres the beautiful one as a god, and if he did not fear to be thought stark mad, he would offer sacrifice to his beloved as to an idol or a god. And as he looks upon him, a reaction from his shuddering comes over him, with sweat and unwonted heat;

english_head: for as the effluence of beauty enters him through the eyes, he is warmed; the effluence moistens the germ of the feathers, and as he grows warm, the parts from which the feathers grow, which were before hard and choked, and prevented the feathers from sprouting, become soft, and as the nourishment streams upon him, the quills of the feathers swell and begin to grow from the roots over all the form of the soul; for it was once all feathered. Now in this process the whole soul throbs and palpitates, and

english_head: as in those who are cutting teeth there is an irritation and discomfort in the gums, when the teeth begin to grow, just so the soul suffers when the growth of the feathers begins; it is feverish and is uncomfortable and itches when they begin to grow. Then when it gazes upon the beauty of the boy and receives the particles which flow thence to it (for which reason they are called yearning), it is moistened and warmed,

So, because of these two mingled sensations,

And when it sees him and is bathed with the waters of yearning, the passages that were sealed are opened, the soul has respite from the stings and is eased of its pain, and this pleasure

Therefore the soul will not, if it can help it, be left alone by the beautiful one, but esteems him above all others, forgets for him mother and brothers and all friends, neglects property and cares not for its loss, and despising all the customs and proprieties in which it formerly took pride, it is ready to be a slave and to sleep wherever it is allowed, as near as possible to the beloved; for it not only reveres him who possesses beauty,

Now this condition, fair boy, about which I am speaking, is called Love by men, but when you hear what the gods call it, perhaps because of your youth you will laugh. But some of the Homeridae, I believe, repeat two verses on Love from the spurious poems of Homer, one of which is very outrageous and not perfectly metrical. They sing them as follows:

Now he who is a follower of Zeus, when seized by love can bear a heavier burden of the winged god; but those who are servants of Ares and followed in his train, when they have been seized by Love and think they have been wronged in any way by the beloved, become murderous and are ready to sacrifice themselves and the beloved.

Now each one chooses his love from the ranks of the beautiful according to his character, and he fashions him and adorns him

The followers of Zeus desire that the soul of him whom they love be like Zeus; so they seek for one of philosophical and lordly nature, and when they find him and love him, they do all they can to give him such a character. If they have not previously had experience, they learn then from all who can teach them anything;

Now they consider the beloved the cause of all this, so they love him more than before, and if they draw the waters of their inspiration from Zeus, like the bacchantes, they pour it out upon the beloved and make him, so far as possible, like their god.

Now of the horses we say one is good and the other bad; but we did not define what the goodness of the one and the badness of the other was. That we must now do. The horse that stands at the right hand is upright and has clean limbs; he carries his neck high, has an aquiline nose, is white in color, and has dark eyes; he is a friend of honor joined with temperance and modesty, and a follower of true glory; he needs no whip, but is guided only by the word of command and by reason.

The effect upon the charioteer is the same as before, but more pronounced; he falls back like a racer from the starting-rope, pulls the bit backward even more violently than before from the teeth of the unruly horse, covers his scurrilous tongue and jaws with blood, and forces his legs and haunches to the ground, causing him much pain. Now when the bad horse has gone through the same experience many times and has ceased from his unruliness, he is humbled and follows henceforth the wisdom of the charioteer, and when he sees the beautiful one, he is overwhelmed with fear; and so from that time on the soul of the lover follows the beloved in reverence and awe.

english_head: and destiny cause him to admit him to his society. For it is the law of fate that evil can never be a friend to evil and that good must always be friend to good. And when the lover is thus admitted, and the privilege of conversation and intimacy has been granted him, his good will, as it shows itself in close intimacy, astonishes the beloved, who discovers that the friendship of all his other friends and relatives is as nothing when compared with that of his inspired lover. And as this intimacy continues and the lover comes near and touches the beloved in the gymnasia and in their general intercourse,

greek_head: τότ' ἤδη ἡ τοῦ ῥεύματος ἐκείνου πηγή ὃν ἵμερον Ζεὺς Γανυμήδους ἐρῶν ὠνόμασε πολλὴ φερομένη πρὸς τὸν
greek_tokens: tote hdh o o reuma ekeinos phgh os imeros zeus ganumhdhs era onomazw polus ferw pros o erasths o men eis autos duw o de apomestoomai ecw aporrew kai oios pneuma h tis hxw apo leiwn te kai stereos allomai palin oqen ormaw ferw outws o o kallos reuma palin eis o kalos dia o omma eimi h fuw epi o yuxh eimi afikneomai kai anapterow
english_head: then the fountain of that stream which Zeus, when he was in love with Ganymede, called desire flows copiously upon the lover; and some of it flows into him, and some, when he is filled, overflows outside; and just as the wind or an echo rebounds from smooth, hard surfaces and returns whence it came, so the stream of beauty passes back into the beautiful one through the eyes, the natural inlet to the soul, where

greek_head: τὰς διόδους τῶν πτερῶν ἄρδει τε καὶ ὥρμησε πτεροφυεῖν τε καὶ τὴν τοῦ ἐρωμένου αὖ ψυχὴν ἔρωτος
greek_tokens: o diodos o pteron ardis te kai ormaw pterofuew te kai o o eraomai au yuxh erws empiplhmi eraomai men oun ostis de aforaw kai oute ostis pasxw oida oude exis frazw alla oios apo allos ofqalmia apolauw profasis eipon ou exis wsper de en katoptron en o eraw eautou oraw lanqanw kai otan men ekeinos pareimi lhgw kata tautos ekeinos o odunh otan de apeimi kata tautos au poqew kai poqew eidwlon
english_head: it reanimates the passages of the feathers, waters them and makes the feathers begin to grow, filling the soul of the loved one with love. So he is in love, but he knows not with whom; he does not understand his own condition and cannot explain it; like one who has caught a disease of the eyes from another, he can give no reason for it; he sees himself in his lover as in a mirror, but is not conscious of the fact. And in the lover’s presence, like him he ceases from his pain, and in his absence, like him he is filled with yearning such as he inspires, and love’s image, requited love, dwells within him;

greek_head: ἔρωτος ἀντέρωτα ἔχων καλεῖ δὲ αὐτὸν καὶ οἴεται οὐκ ἔρωτα ἀλλὰ φιλίαν εἶναι ἐπιθυμεῖ δὲ ἐκείνῳ παραπλησίως μέν
greek_tokens: erws anterws exw kalew de autos kai oiomai ou erws alla filios eimi epiqumew de ekeinos paraplhsios men asqenhs de oraw aptw filew sugkatakeimai kai dh oios eoika poiew o meta outos taxus outos en oun o sugkoimhsis o men erasths o akolastos ippos exis ostis legos pros o hnioxos kai aciow anti polus ponos
english_head: but he calls it, and believes it to be, not love, but friendship. Like the lover, though less strongly, he desires to see his friend, to touch him, kiss him, and lie down by him; and naturally these things are soon brought about. Now as they lie together, the unruly horse of the lover has something to say to the charioteer, and demands a little enjoyment in return for his many troubles;

greek_head: σμικρὰ ἀπολαῦσαι ὁ δὲ τῶν παιδικῶν ἔχει μὲν οὐδὲν εἰπεῖν σπαργῶν δὲ καὶ ἀπορῶν περιβάλλει τὸν ἐραστὴν καὶ φιλεῖ
greek_tokens: mikros apolauw o de o paidikon exis men oudeis eipon spargaw de kai aforaw periballw o erasths kai filew ws sfodra eunous aspazomai otan te sugkatakeimai oios eimi mh an aparneomai o eautou meros xarizomai o eraw ei dew tugxanw o de omozuc au meta o hnioxos pros outos meta aidw kai logos antiteinw ean men dh oun eis tassw te diaita kai filosofia nikaw o beltiwn o dianoia agw makarios men
english_head: and the unruly horse of the beloved says nothing, but teeming with passion and confused emotions he embraces and kisses his lover, caressing him as his best friend; and when they lie together, he would not refuse his lover any favor, if he asked it; but the other horse and the charioteer oppose all this with modesty and reason. If now the better elements of the mind, which lead to a well ordered life and to philosophy, prevail,

greek_head: καὶ ὁμονοητικὸν τὸν ἐνθάδε βίον διάγουσιν ἐγκρατεῖς αὑτῶν καὶ κόσμιοι ὄντες δουλωσάμενοι μὲν ᾧ κακία ψυχῆς ἐνεγίγνετο
greek_tokens: kai omonohtikos o enqade bios diagw egkratew eautou kai kosmios eimi doulow men os kakia yuxazw eggignomai eleuqerow de os areth teleutaw de dh upopteros kai elafros gignomai o treis palaisma o ws alhqhs olumpiakos eis nikaw e megas agaqos oute swfrosunos anqrwpinos oute qeia mania dunatos porizw anqrwpos ean de dh diaita fortikos te kai
english_head: they live a life of happiness and harmony here on earth, self controlled and orderly, holding in subjection that which causes evil in the soul and giving freedom to that which makes for virtue; and when this life is ended they are light and winged, for they have conquered in one of the three truly Olympic contests. Neither human wisdom nor divine inspiration can confer upon man any greater blessing than this. If however they live a life less noble and without philosophy, but yet ruled by the love of honor, probably,

greek_head: ἀφιλοσόφῳ φιλοτίμῳ δὲ χρήσωνται τάχ' ἄν που ἐν μέθαις ἤ τινι ἄλλῃ ἀμελείᾳ τὼ ἀκολάστω αὐτοῖν ὑποζυγίω λαβόντε
greek_tokens: afilosofos filotimos de xraw taxa an pou en meqh h tis allh ameleia o akolastos autos upozeugnumi lambanw o yuxh afrouros sunagw eis tautos o upo o polus makarisths airesis airew te kai diaprassw kai diaprassw o loipos hdh xraomai men autos spanios de ate ou paomai dokew o dianoia prassw filos men oun kai outos hsswn de ekeinos allhlwn
english_head: when they have been drinking, or in some other moment of carelessness, the two unruly horses, taking the souls off their guard, will bring them together and seize upon and accomplish that which is by the many accounted blissful; and when this has once been done, they continue the practice, but infrequently, since what they are doing is not approved by the whole mind. So these two pass through life as friends, though not such friends as the others,

greek_head: διά τε τοῦ ἔρωτος καὶ ἔξω γενομένω διάγουσι πίστεις τὰς μεγίστας ἡγουμένω ἀλλήλοιν δεδωκέναι τε καὶ δεδέχθαι
greek_tokens: dia te o erws kai ecw gignomai diagw pistis o megas hgeomai allhlwn didwmi te kai dexomai os ou qemitos eimi luw eis exqra pote erxomai en de o teleutaw apteros men ormaw de pterow ekbainw o swma wste ou mikros aqlon o erwtikos mania ferw eis gar skotos kai o upo gaia poreia ou nomos eimi eti erxomai o katarxw hdh o upouranios poreia alla fainw bios diagw eudaimonew
english_head: both at the time of their love and afterwards, believing that they have exchanged the most binding pledges of love, and that they can never break them and fall into enmity. And at last, when they depart from the body, they are not winged, to be sure, but their wings have begun to grow, so that the madness of love brings them no small reward; for it is the law that those who have once begun their upward progress shall never again pass into darkness and the journey under the earth, but shall live a happy life in the light as they journey together, and because of their love shall be alike in their plumage when they receive their wings.

greek_head: μετ' ἀλλήλων πορευομένους καὶ ὁμοπτέρους ἔρωτος χάριν ὅταν γένωνται γενέσθαι
greek_tokens: meta allhlwn poreuw kai omopteros erws xaris otan gignomai gignomai outos tosoutos eimi pais kai qeios outws su dwreomai o para erasths filios o de apo o mh eraw oikeioths swfrosunos qnhtos kerannumi qnhtos te kai feidwlh oikonomew aneleuqeria upo plhqos epainew ws areth
english_head: These blessings, so great and so divine, the friendship of a lover will confer upon you, dear boy; but the affection of the non-lover, which is alloyed with mortal prudence and follows mortal and parsimonious rules of conduct, will beget in the beloved soul the narrowness which the common folk praise as virtue; it will cause the soul

greek_head: τῇ φίλῃ ψυχῇ ἐντεκοῦσα ἐννέα χιλιάδας ἐτῶν περὶ γῆν κυλινδουμένην αὐτὴν καὶ ὑπὸ γῆς ἄνουν παρέξει
greek_tokens: o filos yuxw entiktw ennea xilias etai peri gaia kulindew autos kai upo gaia anoos parecis outos su eimi filos eros eis hmeteros dunamis ostis kalos kai aristos didwmi te kai ektinw palinwdia o te allos kai o onoma anagkazw poihtikos tis dia faidros erw alla o proteros te suggnwmh kai ode xaris exw eumenhs kai ilaos o erwtikos egw texnh os didwmi mhte afairew mhte phrow dia orgh didhmi su eti mallon h nun para o kalew timios eimi
english_head: to be a wanderer upon the earth for nine thousand years and a fool below the earth at last. There, dear Love, thou hast my recantation, which I have offered and paid as beautifully and as well as I could, especially in the poetical expressions which I was forced to employ on account of Phaedrus. Pardon, I pray, my former words and accept these words with favor; be kind and gracious to me; do not in anger take from me the art of love which thou didst give me, and deprive me not of sight, but grant unto me to be even more than now esteemed by the beautiful.

greek_head: ἐν τῷ πρόσθεν δ' εἴ τι λόγῳ σοι ἀπηχὲς εἴπομεν Φαῖδρός τε καὶ ἐγώ Λυσίαν τὸν τοῦ λόγου πατέρα αἰτιώμενος παῦε
greek_tokens: en o prosqen de ei tis logos su aphxhs eipon faidros te kai egw lusios o o logos pathr aitiaomai pauw o toioutos logos epi filosofia de wsper adelfos autos polemarxos trepw trepw ina kai o erasths ode autos mhketi epamfoterizw kaqa nun alla aplos pros erws meta filosofos logos o bios poiew suneuxomai su eimi swkraths eiper ameinwn outos
english_head: And if in our former discourse Phaedrus and I said anything harsh against thee, blame Lysias, the father of that discourse, make him to cease from such speeches, and turn him, as his brother Polemarchus is turned, toward philosophy, that his lover Phaedrus may no longer hesitate, as he does now, between two ways, but may direct his life with all singleness of purpose toward love and philosophical discourses. I join in your prayer, Socrates,

greek_head: ἡμῖν εἶναι ταῦτα γίγνεσθαι τὸν λόγον δέ σου πάλαι θαυμάσας ἔχω ὅσῳ καλλίω τοῦ προτέρου ἀπηργάσω ὥστε
greek_tokens: egw eimi outos gignomai o logos de su pala qaumazw exw osos kallias o proteros apergazomai wste oknew mh egw o lusios tapeinos fainw ean ara kai eqelw pros autos allos antiparateinw kai gar tis autos eimi qaumasios enagxos o politikos outos autos loidorew oneidizw kai dia pas o loidoria kalew logografos taxa oun an upo filotimia episxw egw an o grafw geloios ge eimi neanihs o dogma legw kai o
english_head: and pray that this may come to pass, if this is best for us. But all along I have been wondering at your discourse, you made it so much more beautiful than the first; so that I am afraid Lysias will make a poor showing, if he consents to compete with it. Indeed, lately one of the politicians was abusing him for this very thing, and through all his abusive speech kept calling him a speech-writer; so perhaps out of pride he may refrain from writing.

greek_head: ἑταίρου συχνὸν διαμαρτάνεις εἰ αὐτὸν οὕτως ἡγῇ τινα ψοφοδεᾶ ἴσως δὲ καὶ τὸν λοιδορούμενον αὐτῷ οἴει ὀνειδίζοντα
greek_tokens: etairos suxnos diamartanw ei autos outws hgeomai tis yofodehs isos de kai o loidorew autos oiomai oneidizw legw o legw fainw gar eimi swkraths kai sunoida pou kai autos ostis e megas dunamai te kai semnos en o polis aisxunw logos te grafw kai katalimpanw suggramma eautou doca fobew o epeita xronos mh sofisths kalew glukus agkwn eimi faidros lanqanw su ostis apo o
english_head: That is an absurd idea, young man, and you are greatly mistaken in your friend if you think he is so much afraid of noise. Perhaps, too, you think the man who abused him believed what he was saying. He seemed to believe, Socrates; and you know yourself that the most influential and important men in our cities are ashamed to write speeches and leave writings behind them, through fear of being called sophists by posterity. You seem to be unacquainted with the sweet elbow, Phaedrus,

greek_head: μακροῦ ἀγκῶνος τοῦ κατὰ Νεῖλον ἐκλήθη καὶ πρὸς τῷ ἀγκῶνι λανθάνει σε ὅτι οἱ μέγιστον φρονοῦντες τῶν πολιτικῶν
greek_tokens: makros agkwn o kata neilos kalew kai pros o agkwn lanqanw su ostis e megas fronew o politikos malista eraw logografia te kai kataleiyis suggramma e ge kai epeidan tis grafw logos outws agapaw o epaineths wste prosparagrafw proteros e an ekastaxou epainew autos pws legw outos ou gar manqanw
english_head: and besides the elbow, you seem not to know that the proudest of the statesmen are most fond of writing and of leaving writings behind them, since they care so much for praise that when they write a speech they add at the beginning the names of those who praise them in each instance.

greek_head: Οὐ μανθάνεις ὅτι ἐν ἀρχῇ ἀνδρὸς πολιτικοῦ συγγράμματι πρῶτος ὁ ἐπαινέτης γέγραπται
greek_tokens: ou manqanw ostis en arxh anhr politikos suggramma proteros o epaineths grafw pws dokew pou fhmi o bouleus h o dhmos h amfoteros kai os kai os eipon o eautou dh legos mala semnos kai egkwmiazw o suggrafeus epeita legw dh meta outos epideiknumi o epaineths o eautou sofia eniote panu makros poiew suggramma h su allos tis fainw o toioutos h logos suggrafw
english_head: What do you mean? I don’t understand. You don’t understand that the name of the approver is written first in the writings of statesmen. How so? The writer says, It was voted by the senate (or the people, or both), and so-and-so moved, mentioning his own name with great dignity and praise, then after that he goes on, displaying his own wisdom to his approvers, and sometimes making a very long

greek_head: Οὐκ ἔμοιγε Οὐκοῦν ἐὰν μὲν οὗτος ἐμμένῃ γεγηθὼς ἀπέρχεται
greek_tokens: ou egw oukoun ean men outos emmenw ghqew aperxomai ek o qeatron o poihths ean de ecaleifw kai amoiros gignomai logografia te kai o acios eimi suggrafw penqew autos te kai e etairos kai mala dhlos ge ostis ou ws uperfronew o epithdeuma alla ws qaumazw panu men oun tis de otan ikanos gignomai rhtwr h basileus wste
english_head: document. Does it seem to you that a thing of that sort is anything else than a written speech? No, certainly not. Then if this speech is approved, the writer leaves the theater in great delight; but if it is not recorded and he is not granted the privilege of speech-writing and is not considered worthy to be an author, he is grieved, and his friends with him. Decidedly. Evidently not because they despise the profession, but because they admire it. To be sure.

greek_head: λαβὼν τὴν Λυκούργου ἢ Σόλωνος ἢ Δαρείου δύναμιν ἀθάνατος γενέσθαι λογογράφος ἐν πόλει ἆρ' οὐκ ἰσόθεον
greek_tokens: lambanw o lukourgos h solwn h dareios dunamis aqanatos gignomai logografos en polis ara ou isoqeos hgeomai autos te eautou eti zaw kai e epeita gignomai tautos outos peri autos nomizw qeaomai autos o suggramma kai mala oiomai tis oun o toioutos ostis kai opwsoun dusnous lusios oneidizw autos outos ostis suggrafw oukoun eoika ge ek os su legw kai gar an o eautou epiqumia ws eoika oneidizw
english_head: Well then, when an orator or a king is able to rival the greatness of Lycurgus or Solon or Darius and attain immortality as a writer in the state, does he not while living think himself equal to the gods, and has not posterity the same opinion of him, when they see his writings? Very true. Do you think, then, that any of the statesmen, no matter how ill-disposed toward Lysias, reproaches him for being a writer? It is not likely, according to what you say; for he would be casting reproach upon that which he himself desires to be.

greek_head: Τοῦτο μὲν ἄρα παντὶ δῆλον ὅτι οὐκ αἰσχρὸν αὐτό γε τὸ γράφειν λόγους
greek_tokens: outos men ara pas dhlos ostis ou aisxros autos ge o grafw logos tis gar alla ekeinos oimh aisxros hdh o mh kalos legw te kai grafw alla aisxros te kai kakos dhlos dh tis oun o tropos o kalos te kai mh grafw deomai tis eimi faidros lusios te peri outos ecetazw kai allos ostis pwpote tis grafw h grafw eite politikos suggramma eite idiwtikos en metron ws poihths h aneu metron ws idiwths
english_head: Then that is clear to all, that writing speeches is not in itself a disgrace. How can it be? But the disgrace, I fancy, consists in speaking or writing not well, but disgracefully and badly. Evidently. What, then, is the method of writing well or badly? Do we want to question Lysias about this, and anyone else who ever has written or will write anything, whether a public or private document, in verse or in prose, be he poet or ordinary man?

greek_head: Ἐρωτᾷς εἰ δεόμεθα τίνος μὲν οὖν ἕνεκα κἄν τις ὡς εἰπεῖν ζῴη ἀλλ' ἢ τῶν τοιούτων ἡδονῶν ἕνεκα οὐ
greek_tokens: erwtaw ei deomai tis men oun eneka kan tis ws eipon zwh alla h o toioutos hdona eneka ou gar pou ekeinos ge os prolupeomai deomai h mhde hdomai o dh oligos paomai o peri o swma hdona exw dio kai dikaios andrapodwdhs kalew sxolh men dh ws eoika kai ama egw dokew ws en o pnigw uper kefalh egw e tettic aeidw kai
english_head: You ask if we want to question them? What else should one live for, so to speak, but for such pleasures? Certainly not for those which cannot be enjoyed without previous pain, which is the case with nearly all bodily pleasures and causes them to be justly called slavish. We have plenty of time, apparently; and besides, the locusts seem to be looking down upon us as they sing and talk with each other in the heat.

greek_head: ἀλλήλοις διαλεγόμενοι καθορᾶν καὶ ἡμᾶς εἰ οὖν ἴδοιεν καὶ νὼ καθάπερ τοὺς πολλοὺς ἐν μεσημβρίᾳ μὴ διαλεγομένους
greek_tokens: allhlwn dialegw kaqoraw kai egw ei oun eidon kai egw kaqa o polus en meshmbrios mh dialegw alla nustazw kai khlew ufh eautou dia argia o dianoia dikaios an katagelaw hgeomai andrapodon atta sfeis erxomai eis o katagwgion wsper probation meshmbriazw peri o krhnh eudw ean de oraw dialegw kai paraplew sfeis wsper seirhn
english_head: Now if they should see us not conversing at mid-day, but, like most people, dozing, lulled to sleep by their song because of our mental indolence, they would quite justly laugh at us, thinking that some slaves had come to their resort and were slumbering about the fountain at noon like sheep. But if they see us conversing and sailing past them unmoved by the charm of their Siren voices,

greek_head: ἀκηλήτους ὃ γέρας παρὰ θεῶν ἔχουσιν ἀνθρώποις διδόναι τάχ' ἂν δοῖεν ἀγασθέντες
greek_tokens: akhlhtos o geras para qea exw anqrwpos didwmi taxa an didwmi agamai exw de dh tis outos anhkoos gar ws eoika tugxanw eimi ou men dh prepw ge filomousos anhr o toioutos anhkoos eimi legw de os pote eimi outos anqrwpos o prin mousa gignomai gignomai de mousa kai fainw aoidh outws ara tis o tote ekplhssw ufh hdona
english_head: perhaps they will be pleased and give us the gift which the gods bestowed on them to give to men. What is this gift? I don’t seem to have heard of it. It is quite improper for a lover of the Muses never to have heard of such things. The story goes that these locusts were once men, before the birth of the Muses, and when the Muses were born and song appeared, some of the men were so overcome with delight

greek_head: ὥστε ᾄδοντες ἠμέλησαν σίτων τε καὶ ποτῶν καὶ ἔλαθον τελευτήσαντες αὑτούς ἐξ ὧν τὸ τεττίγων γένος μετ' ἐκεῖνο
greek_tokens: wste aeidw amelew sitos te kai poths kai lanqanw teleutaw eautou ek os o tettic genos meta ekeinos fuw geras outos para mousa lambanw mhdeis trefw deomai gignomai alla asitos te kai apotos euqus aeidw ews an teleutaw kai meta outos erxomai para mousa apaggellw tis tis autos timaw o enqade teryixoros men oun o en o xoros timaw autos apaggellw
english_head: that they sang and sang, forgetting food and drink, until at last unconsciously they died. From them the locust tribe afterwards arose, and they have this gift from the Muses, that from the time of their birth they need no sustenance, but sing continually, without food or drink, until they die, when they go to the Muses and report who honors each of them on earth. They tell Terpsichore of those who have honored her in dances, and make them dearer to her;

greek_head: ποιοῦσι προσφιλεστέρους τῇ δὲ Ἐρατοῖ τοὺς ἐν τοῖς ἐρωτικοῖς καὶ ταῖς ἄλλαις οὕτως κατὰ τὸ εἶδος ἑκάστης
greek_tokens: poiew prosfilhs o de eratw o en o erwtikos kai o allos outws kata o eidos ekastos timaw o de presbus kallioph kai o meta autos ouranios o en filosofia diagw te kai timaw o ekeinos mousikh aggellw o dh malista o mousa peri te ouranos kai logos eimi qeios te kai anqrwpinos ihmi kalos fwnh polus dh oun eneka lekteos tis kai ou kaqeudhteon en o meshmbrios lekteos gar oun
english_head: they gain the favor of Erato for the poets of love, and that of the other Muses for their votaries, according to their various ways of honoring them; and to Calliope, the eldest of the Muses, and to Urania who is next to her, they make report of those who pass their lives in philosophy and who worship these Muses who are most concerned with heaven and with thought divine and human and whose music is the sweetest. So for many reasons we ought to talk and not sleep in the noontime. Yes, we ought to talk.

greek_head: Οὐκοῦν ὅπερ νῦν προυθέμεθα σκέψασθαι τὸν λόγον ὅπῃ καλῶς ἔχει λέγειν τε καὶ γράφειν καὶ ὅπῃ μή σκεπτέον
greek_tokens: oukoun osper nun protiqhmi skeptomai o logos oph kalos exis legw te kai grafw kai oph mh skepteos dhlos ara oun ou uparxw deomai o eu ge kai kalos erw o o legw dianoia oida o alhqhs os an erew peri mellw outwsi peri outos akouw eimi filos swkraths ou
english_head: We should, then, as we were proposing just now, discuss the theory of good (or bad) speaking and writing. Clearly. If a speech is to be good, must not the mind of the speaker know the truth about the matters of which he is to speak?

english_head: On that point, Socrates, I have heard that one who is to be an orator does not need to know what is really just, but what would seem just to the multitude who are to pass judgment, and not what is really good or noble, but what will seem to be so; for they say that persuasion comes from what seems to be true, not from the truth. Phaedrus, The word,which the wise speak must not be rejected, but we must see if they are right; so we must not pass by this which you just said. You are right. Let us then examine it in this way. How?

In the case, then, of those whose opinions are at variance with facts and who are deceived, this error evidently slips in through some resemblances. It does happen in that way. Then he who does not understand the real nature of things will not possess the art of making his hearers pass from one thing to its opposite by leading them through the intervening resemblances, or of avoiding such deception himself? Never in the world.

It is clear to everyone that we are in accord about some matters of this kind and at variance about others, is it not? I think I understand your meaning, but express it still more clearly. When one says iron or silver, we all understand the same thing, do we not? Surely. What if he says justice or goodness? Do we not part company, and disagree with each other and with ourselves? Certainly.

english_head: Then in some things we agree and in others we do not. True. Then in which of the two are we more easy to deceive, and in which has rhetoric the greater power? Evidently in the class of doubtful things. Then he who is to develop an art of rhetoric must first make a methodical division and acquire a clear impression of each class, that in which people must be in doubt and that in which they are not.

I thought you were going to speak the truth and say madly; however, that is just what I had in mind. We said that love was a kind of madness, did we not? Yes. And that there are two kinds of madness, one arising from human diseases, and the other from a divine release from the customary habits.

english_head: The method of the art of healing is much the same as that of rhetoric. How so? In both cases you must analyze a nature, in one that of the body and in the other that of the soul, if you are to proceed in a scientific manner, not merely by practice and routine, to impart health and strength to the body by prescribing medicine and diet, or by proper discourses and training to give to the soul the desired belief and virtue. That, Socrates, is probably true.

If Hippocrates the Asclepiad is to be trusted, one cannot know the nature of the body, either, except in that way. He is right, my friend; however, we ought not to be content with the authority of Hippocrates, but to see also if our reason agrees with him on examination. I assent. Then see what

Of course.

english_head: Then this is the goal of all his effort; he tries to produce conviction in the soul. Is not that so? Yes. So it is clear that Thrasymachus, or anyone else who seriously teaches the art of rhetoric, will first describe the soul with perfect accuracy and make us see whether it is one and all alike, or, like the body, of multiform aspect; for this is what we call explaining its nature. Certainly. And secondly he will say what its action is and toward what it is directed, or how it is acted upon and by what. To be sure.

english_head: to lead souls by persuasion, he who is to be a rhetorician must know the various forms of soul. Now they are so and so many and of such and such kinds, wherefore men also are of different kinds: these we must classify. Then there are also various classes of speeches, to one of which every speech belongs. So men of a certain sort are easily persuaded by speeches of a certain sort for a certain reason to actions or beliefs of a certain sort, and men of another sort cannot be so persuaded. The student of rhetoric must, accordingly, acquire a proper knowledge of these classes and then be able to follow them

english_head: and now you, who are the father of letters, have been led by your affection to ascribe to them a power the opposite of that which they really possess. For this invention will produce forgetfulness in the minds of those who learn to use it, because they will not practice their memory. Their trust in writing, produced by external characters which are no part of themselves, will discourage the use of their own memory within them. You have invented an elixir not of memory, but of reminding; and you offer your pupils the appearance of wisdom, not true wisdom, for they will read many things without instruction and will therefore seem

And so it is with written words; you might think they spoke as if they had intelligence, but if you question them, wishing to know about their sayings, they always say only one and the same thing. You are quite right about that, too.

english_head: Now tell me; is there not another kind of speech, or word, which shows itself to be the legitimate brother of this bastard one, both in the manner of its begetting and in its better and more powerful nature? What is this word and how is it begotten, as you say? The word which is written with intelligence in the mind of the learner, which is able to defend itself and knows to whom it should speak, and before whom to be silent. You mean the living and breathing word of him who knows, of which the written word may justly be called the image.

english_head: Exactly. Now tell me this. Would a sensible husbandman, who has seeds which he cares for and which he wishes to bear fruit, plant them with serious purpose in the heat of summer in some garden of Adonis, and delight in seeing them appear in beauty in eight days, or would he do that sort of thing, when he did it at all, only in play and for amusement? Would he not, when he was in earnest, follow the rules of husbandry, plant his seeds in fitting ground, and be pleased when those which he had sowed reached their perfection in the eighth month?

english_head: Yes, Socrates, he would, as you say, act in that way when in earnest and in the other way only for amusement. And shall we suppose that he who has knowledge of the just and the good and beautiful has less sense about his seeds than the husbandman? By no means. Then he will not, when in earnest, write them in ink, sowing them through a pen with words which cannot defend themselves by argument and cannot teach the truth effectually. No, at least, probably not.

english_head: No. The gardens of letters he will, it seems, plant for amusement, and will write, when he writes, to treasure up reminders for himself, when he comes to the forgetfulness of old age, and for others who follow the same path, and he will be pleased when he sees them putting forth tender leaves. When others engage in other amusements, refreshing themselves with banquets and kindred entertainments, he will pass the time in such pleasures as I have suggested.
